name: ci-path-selection
whenToUse: Decide which CI lanes a change actually needs so heavy runs can be skipped for irrelevant diffs — to cut CI time/cost without weakening correctness. Maps changed paths to required vs skippable lanes, fail-open.
workflow: Get the changed paths: git diff --name-only <base>... (or the PR file list).
Run: git diff --name-only origin/main... | python3 tools/ci_path_select.py --stdin --json
Read 'required' (must run) vs 'skippable' (safe to skip). fast-ci is ALWAYS required.
FAIL-OPEN: any .py / data / requirements / unknown path forces the full heavy suite; only pure docs, skill-registry JSON, or workflow-YAML diffs skip heavy lanes.

To act on it: drive the ci.yml dorny/paths-filter outputs from the same path rules, or gate each heavy job's `if:` on the matching filter — but keep fast-ci + lint_claims + the required status gate always-on, and keep the gate green-on-skip.
Never skip a lane that a changed path could affect; when unsure, run it. Report time saved as best-effort, not guaranteed coverage.
verification: fast-ci (lint_claims, attribution, compileall, dependency-free unit tests) is in 'required' for every input, including empty.
Any .py / requirements / pyproject / data path yields full=true (no skippable).
Unknown/unrecognised paths and an empty diff fail open to the full suite.
Only docs / skills/registry/*.json / .github/workflows/*.yml diffs mark heavy lanes skippable.
The required status check still passes when skipped lanes are intentionally not run (green-on-skip).
commonFailures: Fail-closed misconfig: skipping a lane a change actually affects → false-green merge (the cardinal sin here).
Skipping the cheap fast-ci / lint gate to save trivial time.
Path globs that don't match (e.g. leading ./ or .github dot) so nothing is classified.
Letting a 'docs-only' PR that also touches a .py file skip heavy lanes (union must force full).

examples.output: required: fast-ci; skippable: all heavy lanes.
required: fast-ci, validate-core, test; skip reasoning/build/safety.
full suite (python forces all heavy lanes).
